Active Work Architecture
─[ Field Fix Contribution Pipeline ]─ ┐

Field Fix Contribution Pipeline

Vision

Lore instances discover and fix bugs during normal operation via field-repair. Today those fixes stay local. This roadmap builds a pipeline where deployed instances contribute fixes back to the source framework, with AI-assisted triage and quality gates on the receiving end.

This is a novel capability: deployed software that contributes fixes back to its own source, with AI-powered selection when multiple instances fix the same bug differently.

Architecture

Three-layer design:

  1. Instance packaging — Instances structure field fixes with metadata (context, diff, test evidence, security self-check) and submit via gh CLI
  2. Source triage — GitHub Actions intake pipeline classifies, labels, detects duplicates, and runs convention-aware AI review
  3. Feedback loop — When multiple fixes target the same root cause, AI compares implementations and recommends the best one

Design Decisions

  • Operator-approved submissions — No automatic submission. The instance operator reviews and approves every fix before it leaves the instance.
  • Claude Code Action for triage — anthropics/claude-code-action@v1 powers the GitHub Actions intake and review pipelines.
  • Full AI-powered fix selection — When duplicate fixes exist, AI compares and recommends the best implementation. Human maintainer makes the final merge decision.

Phases

PhaseTitleDescriptionDepends on
1Field Fix Metadata FormatStructured metadata schema — the contract between instances and source—
2Field Fix Submit SkillInstance-side /field-fix-submit skill for packaging and submitting fixesPhase 1
3Intake & Triage WorkflowGitHub Actions pipeline for classification, labeling, and duplicate detectionPhase 1
4AI Review GateConvention-aware AI architecture review on field-fix PRsPhase 3
5Fix Grouping & AI SelectionDuplicate detection and AI-powered implementation comparisonPhase 3, 4

Key Constraints

  • Cross-platform — Must work on Linux, macOS, and Windows (WSL)
  • Cross-tool — Submit skill must work from Claude Code, Cursor, and OpenCode instances
  • Security — No instance-specific data (operator identity, local paths, env vars, secrets) may leak into submissions
  • Convention enforcement — All submitted fixes must pass coding and security convention checks
  • Ownership boundaries — Only harness-owned files (hooks, scripts, lib, skills) are valid submission targets

Prior Art

The Contribution Governance and Workflow brainstorm explored three contribution models and recommended Model B (fork source, fix in source) for external contributors. This roadmap builds on that foundation with the instance-to-source direction and adds the AI triage/selection layers that weren’t previously scoped.

Success Criteria

  • Field fixes flow from instances to source repo as structured PRs
  • AI triage classifies and labels incoming fixes without human intervention
  • Duplicate fixes are detected and grouped automatically
  • AI review enforces coding and security conventions before human review
  • When multiple fixes exist for the same bug, AI recommends the best implementation
  • No instance-specific data appears in any submission

Open Questions

  1. CLA/DCO — Do field-fix submissions need a Contributor License Agreement or Developer Certificate of Origin? Deferred from the original brainstorm.
  2. Cross-instance testing — How to verify a fix from one instance doesn’t break others? CI matrix per Phase 3.
  3. Embedding infrastructure — Phase 5 needs semantic similarity. Evaluate: Anthropic API embeddings, local FastEmbed, or heuristic grouping as MVP.
  4. Rate limiting — If many instances submit fixes simultaneously, does the triage pipeline need throttling?
